threats.title: Permanent Fund Lock due to Missing userTotalStaked Update in release()
threats.hypothesis_description: The release() function correctly restores a user's individual stake.amount but fails to increment their aggregate userTotalStaked balance. This creates a permanent accounting desynchronization that causes subsequent withdrawals to revert due to arithmetic underflow.
threats.investigation_rationale: State inconsistency between individual stake amounts and the aggregate userTotalStaked mapping directly impacts the withdrawal logic, leading to guaranteed transaction reverts when users attempt to withdraw their restored funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State update omission: selfStakes[staker].amount is increased but userTotalStaked[staker] is not

preconditions: RELEASER_ROLE calls release() to restore funds
User attempts to withdraw the restored funds

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: userTotalStaked is decremented in slash() but not incremented in release()
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No synchronization between individual stakes and total staked

analysis_questions: Does any other function correct the userTotalStaked balance?
Can the user bypass the underflow by staking more?
validation_requirements: Write a test case simulating stake -> slash -> release -> withdraw
Verify the underflow revert on withdrawal

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. User stakes 100 GTC. 2. SLASHER slashes 50 GTC (userTotalStaked becomes 50). 3. RELEASER releases 50 GTC (stake amount becomes 100, userTotalStaked remains 50). 4. User calls withdrawSelfStake(100). 5. Transaction reverts due to 50 - 100 underflow.
prerequisites: Successful appeal and release of funds

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent lock of released funds in the contract
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users who successfully appeal a slash

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing end-to-end test for slash -> release -> withdraw flow

threats.title: Missing Allowance Validation in GTC Test Token Allows Arbitrary Fund Theft
threats.hypothesis_description: The GTC.sol test mock intentionally removes the allowance check in transferFrom(). While intended for testing, if this mock is ever deployed to a live testnet or production environment, any user can steal all tokens from any other user or contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Test mocks with disabled security checks pose a critical risk if accidentally deployed or used in staging environments.
deployment_assumptions: The GTC.sol mock is deployed instead of the real GTC token
threats.config_evidence: GTC.sol line 200: '// Removed allowance check for easy testing'
preconditions: GTC.sol mock is used in the deployed environment

analysis_questions: Is this mock used in any public testnet deployments?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Attacker calls GTC.transferFrom(IdentityStaking, attacker, contractBalance). 2. Transaction succeeds because allowance is not checked. 3. Attacker drains all staked funds.

threats.title: Uninitialized UUPS Implementation Can Be Hijacked and Destroyed
threats.hypothesis_description: The IdentityStaking contract lacks a constructor with _disableInitializers(). This allows an attacker to directly initialize the implementation contract, grant themselves admin rights, and upgrade it to a self-destructing contract, permanently bricking the proxy.
threats.investigation_rationale: Standard UUPS security requires disabling initializers on the implementation contract to prevent direct interaction. Missing this exposes the logic contract to takeover and destruction.
preconditions: Contract is deployed as a UUPS implementation
Implementation contract is not manually initialized by the deployer

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Implementation can be initialized by anyone at any time

analysis_questions: Is the implementation contract initialized during deployment?
Does the specific OpenZeppelin version used automatically protect against this?
validation_requirements: Attempt to upgrade implementation to selfdestruct

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Attacker monitors for IdentityStaking implementation deployment. 2. Attacker calls initialize() directly on the implementation. 3. Attacker calls upgradeToAndCall() with a malicious contract that executes selfdestruct. 4. Implementation is destroyed; proxy is permanently bricked.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete loss of all funds locked in the proxy

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for direct implementation initialization

threats.title: Multiplication Overflow in slash() Grants Slashing Immunity to Large Stakes
threats.hypothesis_description: The slash() function multiplies percent (uint88) by amount (uint88) before dividing by 100. For stakes > 3.09M GTC, this multiplication overflows type(uint88).max, causing the transaction to revert and making the stake immune to high-percentage slashes.
threats.investigation_rationale: Solidity 0.8.x checks for arithmetic overflow. Since both operands are uint88, the multiplication is performed in uint88, leading to a guaranteed revert for large values.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Multiplication before division with narrow integer types: (percent * selfStakes[staker].amount) / 100

preconditions: User stakes > 3.09M GTC (approx 3% of total supply)
SLASHER_ROLE attempts to slash the user at 100%

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No casting to uint256 before multiplication

analysis_questions: What is the exact token amount threshold for overflow at 100% slash?
Can the SLASHER bypass this by slashing in smaller increments?
validation_requirements: Test slash() with a stake of 4M GTC and 100% percent

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Malicious whale stakes 4,000,000 GTC. 2. Whale misbehaves. 3. SLASHER calls slash() with 100%. 4. Transaction reverts due to overflow. 5. Whale withdraws funds after lock expires, escaping penalty.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of Sybil resistance and penalty mechanism for large holders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade or many small-percentage slashes

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for slashing very large stake amounts

threats.title: Unvalidated burnAddress Can Permanently Brick Slashing Mechanism
threats.hypothesis_description: The initialize() function does not validate that _burnAddress is non-zero. If deployed with address(0), and the underlying token reverts on zero-address transfers (like GTC does), the lockAndBurn() function will permanently revert, halting all slash round progressions.
threats.investigation_rationale: Configuration errors in immutable or hard-to-change variables can lead to permanent DoS of core protocol mechanisms.
threats.config_evidence: No check for _burnAddress == address(0) in initialize()

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing zero-address validation for critical state variable

preconditions: Token reverts on transfer to address(0)
There are slashed funds to burn

analysis_questions: What is the intended burn address?
Can the admin upgrade the contract to fix this?

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Contract deployed with burnAddress = address(0). 2. Users are slashed. 3. 90 days pass. 4. Anyone calls lockAndBurn(). 5. Transaction reverts because GTC token rejects transfer to address(0). 6. Slashing rounds can never advance.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Slashed funds are stuck in the contract forever instead of being burned
